messages.content: what is java project jigsaw

Java Project Jigsaw is a modular system introduced in Java 9 that allows developers to modularize their code, which means organizing code into logical, independent units that can be easily developed, tested, and maintained. It enables developers to create a modular application that has a well-defined, cohesive structure. Jigsaw provides a set of tools and APIs that allow developers to develop, manage, and distribute modular applications. It also helps improve security, performance, and scalability by making it easier to manage dependencies between different components of an application.
is java 9 backward compatible

Java 9 is generally not considered to be fully backward compatible with earlier Java versions. This is because Java 9 introduced a number of changes and new features that may cause compatibility issues with older applications. These changes include the introduction of the Java Module System (Jigsaw), changes to the default garbage collector, and several new APIs.

However, to address this issue, Java 9 does provide a set of tools to help developers migrate their applications to the new module system and to maintain backward compatibility. This includes the "jdeps" tool, which analyzes a program and determines its dependencies on specific classes and modules. Additionally, Java 9 includes the "jdk.unsupported" module that can be used to access deprecated classes and features that have been removed from Java 9.

Overall, while Java 9 is not fully backward compatible, with the proper tools and techniques, developers can still ensure their applications continue to work with newer versions of Java.
is java still free to use ?

Yes, Java can still be used for free. It is an open source programming language and platform, and the Java Development Kit (JDK) and Java Runtime Environment (JRE) are available for free download from Oracle's website.

However, Oracle, which is the company that owns Java, has made some changes to its licensing policy in recent years. As of Java 11, Oracle has transitioned to a new release cycle and support model for Java, which means that certain updates and features are only available to those who have purchased a support subscription. This mainly affects enterprise users who require long-term support and security updates beyond the standard six-month release cycle.

But for regular users and small-scale projects, Java can still be used and distributed for free. It's important to read and understand the terms and conditions of the Oracle Binary Code License Agreement when downloading and using Java.
what is the last version of java ee

Java EE (Enterprise Edition) has been rebranded as Jakarta EE, and the last version of Java EE before the rebranding was Java EE 8, which was released in September 2017. 

After the rebranding, the first version of Jakarta EE was Jakarta EE 8, which was released in September 2019. Jakarta EE 8 is essentially the same as Java EE 8, but with the addition of a new namespace and packaging convention to reflect the name change from "Java" to "Jakarta". 

It's important to note that Jakarta EE is not a new platform or technology; it is a rebranding of Java EE and continues to provide the same enterprise capabilities and programming model as Java EE.
why was java ee rebranded into jakarta ee

Java EE (Enterprise Edition) was rebranded into Jakarta EE due to a trademark dispute between Oracle, which owns the Java trademark, and the Eclipse Foundation, which has been working to promote and develop Java EE.

Oracle and the Eclipse Foundation had been working together to move Java EE to an open-source foundation, and in 2017, the Eclipse Foundation announced that it would be taking over the stewardship of Java EE. However, this was complicated by a trademark issue, as Oracle retained the rights to the "Java" name.

To resolve this issue, the Eclipse Foundation decided to rename the platform to Jakarta EE. This decision was made after consulting with the Java community, including vendors, developers, and users, and it was chosen because the name Jakarta has a strong association with the open-source community and was available for use as a trademark.

By renaming Java EE to Jakarta EE, the Eclipse Foundation was able to gain full control over the platform's development and direction, while still maintaining compatibility with existing Java EE applications and investments.
what does jakarta mean

"Jakarta" is the capital city of Indonesia, which is a country in Southeast Asia. The city was formerly known as Batavia, and was renamed Jakarta in 1949, after Indonesia declared independence from the Netherlands.

As a name, "Jakarta" has no specific meaning in the context of technology or software development. The Eclipse Foundation chose the name mainly because it was available for trademark use, and because it has a strong association with the open-source community due to projects like the Apache Jakarta project.

The renaming of Java EE to Jakarta EE was done to reflect the change in stewardship and the move to an open-source foundation, and to differentiate it from the Oracle-owned Java brand.
what apache jakarta

Apache Jakarta is a project of the Apache Software Foundation that used to provide a set of open-source Java-based solutions for building, testing, and deploying enterprise applications. Jakarta was once a top-level project and has now been subsumed under the Apache Tomcat project.

The Jakarta project was originally created by Sun Microsystems as a set of Java-based libraries and tools for building enterprise-level applications, and it was later donated to the Apache Software Foundation. Since then, the project has been expanded to include multiple sub-projects focused on various aspects of enterprise application development, such as servlets, JSP, JavaMail, and more.

Some of the notable sub-projects of Apache Jakarta included:
- Apache Tomcat: A web server and servlet container
- Apache Struts: A popular web application framework for Java
- Apache Ant: A build automation tool for Java projects
- Apache Maven: A project management and build automation tool for Java applications

The Jakarta project was merged into the Apache Tomcat project to become the Apache Tomcat project management committee in 2011, with Tomcat as its flagship product. Today, several of the Jakarta sub-projects are still actively maintained and used in enterprise Java development.
what is the last version of jakarta ee

The last version of Jakarta EE (Enterprise Edition) as of August 2021 is Jakarta EE 9.1. This version of Jakarta EE is based on the Java EE 8 specification and includes updates and improvements made by the Jakarta community since the renaming of Java EE to Jakarta EE.

Some of the notable changes in Jakarta EE 9.1 include updates to the Jakarta RESTful Web Services specification, support for Java SE 11 as the minimum runtime version for Jakarta EE applications, and improvements to the Jakarta Enterprise Beans specification.

It's worth noting that while Jakarta EE 9.1 is the latest version, it is an incremental release of Jakarta EE 9, which was the first release after the rebranding from Java EE. Both Jakarta EE 9 and 9.1 are designed to be fully compatible with Java EE 8, so applications written for Java EE 8 should be able to run on Jakarta EE with little or no modification.
is it possible to use jdk 6 still

While it is technically possible to use JDK 6 (Java Development Kit 6) for software development, it is no longer recommended, as Java 6 has reached its end-of-life (EOL) and is no longer supported or updated with security patches by Oracle. This means that using Java 6 could leave your applications vulnerable to security exploits and other issues.

Additionally, even if you choose to use JDK 6 for development, you might encounter compatibility issues with modern software libraries and tools, which are often designed to work with the latest versions of Java.

It's important to note that while Oracle no longer provides public updates, other vendors and organizations may still offer support for Java 6 for internal use. However, it's generally recommended to use a more up-to-date version of Java, such as Java 8 or higher, for new software development, as these versions offer better performance, security, and support for modern features and libraries.
does intellij idea support jdk 6 still

IntelliJ IDEA is a popular Java development environment that supports multiple versions of JDK (Java Development Kit) for software development, including JDK 6. However, support for Java 6 in IntelliJ IDEA may vary depending on the version you're using.

For example, the latest version of IntelliJ IDEA as of August 2021, which is IntelliJ IDEA 2021.2, officially supports JDK 8 and newer versions. However, previous versions of IntelliJ IDEA, such as IntelliJ IDEA 15, IntelliJ IDEA 14, and IntelliJ IDEA 13, still support Java 6 in some capacity.

It's worth noting that while IntelliJ IDEA may support Java 6, as mentioned earlier, Java 6 is no longer supported by Oracle and may have compatibility issues with modern libraries and tools. Therefore, it's recommended to use a more recent version of Java for new software development, even if your development environment supports Java 6.
which IDE can still be used to code in java 6

Most of the modern Java IDEs support Java 6 development with varying degrees of compatibility and integration. However, some IDEs that are known to support Java 6 include:

1. Eclipse: Eclipse is an open-source Java IDE that is known for its extensive support of multiple Java versions. It supports Java 6 and also offers plugins like the “Eclipse Java 6 Support” plugin which enhances the Java 6 development capabilities.

2. IntelliJ IDEA: As mentioned earlier, certain versions of IntelliJ IDEA, such as IntelliJ IDEA 15, IntelliJ IDEA 14, and IntelliJ IDEA 13, support Java 6.

3. NetBeans: NetBeans is another popular open-source Java IDE that provides support for a range of Java versions, including Java 6. It also has a range of features for code editing, debugging, GUI building, and database development.

It is important to note that, while these IDEs support Java 6 development, it is recommended to use a more recent version of Java for new software development as Java 6 has reached its end-of-life and is no longer updated by Oracle.
